title: Chinese Egg Fried Rice

ingredients.name: cooked rice (day-old, cold)
visual_checks: cold leftover rice fries better; freshly cooked rice can be spread on a tray and cooled in the fridge for 30 minutes
ingredients.notes: cold leftover rice fries better; freshly cooked rice can be spread on a tray and cooled in the fridge for 30 minutes

subs.name: cooked jasmine rice or sona masoori rice
subs.notes: sona masoori works perfectly; ensure it is cold

ingredients.name: spring onions, thinly sliced (white and green parts separated)

subs.name: 2 tbsp soy sauce + 1 tsp salt if soy sauce is unavailable
subs.notes: use less salt if the soy is already salty

subs.name: a few drops of mustard oil for a pungent finish
subs.notes: sesame oil is preferred for authenticity

subs.name: diced carrot or corn kernels
subs.notes: add carrot 1 minute earlier as it takes longer to cook

steps.text: Beat the eggs with a pinch of salt and set aside. Break up any clumps in the cold rice with your fingers so the grains are loose.
Heat a wok or wide kadai over the highest flame possible until it just begins to smoke. Add 2 tablespoons of oil and swirl to coat.
Pour in the beaten eggs. Leave them for 10 seconds until the edges set, then scramble them quickly until just cooked but still soft. Remove the eggs to a plate.
Add the remaining 1 tablespoon of oil to the same hot wok. Add garlic and the white parts of the spring onion. Stir-fry for 30 seconds until fragrant — do not let them brown.
Tip in the peas if using, and stir for 1 minute until heated through.

Add all the cold rice at once. Press it down with the spatula and let it sit on the heat for 30 seconds to pick up colour, then toss and repeat. Continue for 3–4 minutes until each grain is hot and slightly golden.
Season with soy sauce and pepper. Toss well so every grain is coated. Return the scrambled eggs, breaking them into bite-size pieces as you fold them in.
Remove from heat, drizzle with sesame oil, and finish with the green parts of the spring onion. Serve immediately.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: rice → cooked jasmine rice or sona masoori rice (sona masoori works perfectly; ensure it is cold); soy-sauce → 2 tbsp soy sauce + 1 tsp salt if soy sauce is unavailable (use less salt if the soy is already salty); gingelly-oil → a few drops of mustard oil for a pungent finish (sesame oil is preferred for authenticity); peas → diced carrot or corn kernels (add carrot 1 minute earlier as it takes longer to cook)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A wide kadai on the highest gas flame replicates wok heat well — the key is not overcrowding. Use day-old ponni or sona masoori rice. Gingelly oil (nallennai) is your sesame oil; find it in any South Indian grocery. Light soy sauce is available at most Indian supermarkets and Big Bazaar under brands like Chings or Maggi.
